A confirmation dialog for destructive or irreversible actions. Unlike Dialog, clicking outside or pressing Escape does not dismiss it — the user must explicitly choose an action. Use it to guard any action that cannot be undone.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { AlertDialogDemo } from "../../../components/DialogDemo.tsx"; ## Default \n \n {(props) => }\n \n \n \n \n Delete project?\n \n This will permanently delete the project and all its audits.\n This action cannot be undone.\n \n Cancel\n Delete project\n \n \n`} center > ## Submitting a form on confirm When the confirmed action needs to submit to a server, use `useSubmit` from React Router in the `AlertDialog.Action`'s `onClick` handler. This avoids a hidden form and keeps the intent explicit. 
{`import { useSubmit } from 'react-router';
import { AlertDialog, Button } from '@eekodigital/raster';

function DeleteProjectButton({ project }) {
const submit = useSubmit();
return (



{(props) => }




Delete project?

"{project.name}" and all its audits will be permanently
deleted. This cannot be undone.

Cancel

submit({ \_intent: 'delete' }, { method: 'post' })
} >
Delete project




);
}`}
## When to use

Use AlertDialog for: deleting records, revoking access, bulk destructive operations, publishing irreversible changes.

Don't use AlertDialog for: routine confirmations, navigating away from unsaved forms (use a beforeunload guard instead), or anything reversible — those suit a Toast undo pattern better.

## Sub-components
Component Description
AlertDialog.Root State provider. No visual output.
AlertDialog.Trigger The element that opens the dialog. Pass a render prop to render as a Button.
AlertDialog.Portal Renders overlay and content into document.body.
AlertDialog.Overlay Dimmed backdrop. Clicking it does not close the dialog.
AlertDialog.Content The dialog panel. Focus is trapped here while open.
AlertDialog.Title Required. Names the dialog for assistive technology.
AlertDialog.Description Required. Explains the consequences of the action.
AlertDialog.Cancel Styled dismiss button. Closes without taking action.
AlertDialog.Action Styled confirm button. Wire up your action in onClick.
A circular (or square) representation of a user or organisation. Built on Radix Avatar for graceful image loading — shows the image when available, falls back to initials otherwise.

Radix handles the load/error state and shows the `Fallback` if the image fails. \n \n JD\n`} center > ## Sizes Three sizes: `sm` (2rem), `md` (2.5rem, default), `lg` (3.5rem). SM\nMD\nLG`} center > ## Square Use `square` for organisation logos and non-person avatars. OG`} center > ## Props ### Avatar.Root

size "sm" | "md" | "lg" "md" Diameter of the avatar.
square boolean false Rounded square instead of circle.
className string — Additional CSS classes.
### Avatar.Image
Prop Type Description
src string Image URL.
alt string Accessible alt text.
### Avatar.Fallback
Prop Type Default Description
children ReactNode — Typically 1–2 initials.
delayMs number 600 Delay before fallback renders (avoids flash on slow images).

A compact pill for counts, statuses, and numeric indicators. Badges are inline and designed to sit alongside labels, nav items, or table cells. For longer text labels and audit statuses, use Tag instead.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { BadgeVariantsDemo, BadgeInContextDemo } from "../../../components/BadgeDemo.tsx"; ## Variants 12\nNew\nPassed\n3\n7`} > ## In context Badges typically appear alongside a label to communicate counts or states at a glance. \n
\n Open findings\n 14\n
\n
\n Pending review\n 7\n
\n
\n Passed controls\n 98\n
\n
\n Not applicable\n 5\n
\n`} > ## When to use

Use Badge for compact numeric counts and single-word statuses that sit inline alongside a label, nav item, or table cell — unread messages, open findings, pass/fail indicators.

Use Tag instead {" "} for longer text labels or multi-word statuses where a pill with more breathing room is appropriate.

## Props
Prop Type Default Description
variant "neutral" | "primary" | "success" | "danger" | "warning" "neutral" Visual intent.

Save report\n\n`} > ### Secondary Supporting or lower-priority actions. Cancel\n`} > ### Danger Destructive or irreversible actions. Delete project`}> ## Disabled state Save report\n`} > ## Render prop

Why does this exist? An <a> inside a{" "} <button> is invalid HTML. Passing a render prop applies button styles to any element — typically a router{" "} Link — so the rendered HTML is a single <a>, not a button wrapping a link.

A controlled disclosure component built on Radix Collapsible. The trigger and content are separate sub-components that can be positioned independently, and state can be driven programmatically via open / onOpenChange.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { CollapsibleBasicDemo, CollapsibleOpenDemo } from "../../../components/CollapsibleDemo.tsx";

Collapsible vs Details. Use Details (native HTML) for simple disclosure patterns — FAQs, inline help, "show more" — where you don't need animation or programmatic control. It's zero-JS and browser-managed. Use Collapsible when you need: controlled state (open{" "} + onOpenChange), animated open/close via data-state, or trigger and content that aren't co-located in the markup (e.g. a sidebar toggle button controlling a panel elsewhere). Accordion already uses Collapsible internally.

## Basic (uncontrolled) \n Audit scope\n \n This audit covers financial controls…\n \n`} > ## Open by default \n Findings summary\n …\n`} > ## Controlled Pass `open` and `onOpenChange` to drive state from your component. ```tsx const [open, setOpen] = useState(false); Toggle … ; ``` ## Props ### Collapsible.Root
Prop Type Default Description
open boolean — Controlled open state.
defaultOpen boolean false Uncontrolled initial state.
onOpenChange (open: boolean) => void — Called when state changes.
disabled boolean false Prevents toggling.
className string — Additional CSS classes.
A data grid backed by TanStack Table v8. Supports multi-column sorting and optional client-side pagination. Use it when Table's single-column sort or lack of pagination isn't enough.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { DataTable as DS_DataTable } from "@eekodigital/raster/data-table"; export const rows = [ { id: "1", ref: "1.1.1", level: "A", name: "Non-text Content", status: "pass" }, { id: "2", ref: "1.2.1", level: "A", name: "Audio-only and Video-only", status: "todo" }, { id: "3", ref: "1.2.2", level: "A", name: "Captions (Prerecorded)", status: "fail" }, { id: "4", ref: "1.3.1", level: "A", name: "Info and Relationships", status: "pass" }, { id: "5", ref: "1.3.2", level: "A", name: "Meaningful Sequence", status: "pass" }, { id: "6", ref: "1.4.1", level: "A", name: "Use of Color", status: "todo" }, { id: "7", ref: "1.4.3", level: "AA", name: "Contrast (Minimum)", status: "pass" }, { id: "8", ref: "2.1.1", level: "A", name: "Keyboard", status: "pass" }, ]; export const columns = [ { accessorKey: "ref", header: "Ref" }, { accessorKey: "name", header: "Name" }, { accessorKey: "level", header: "Level" }, { accessorKey: "status", header: "Status" }, ]; export const wideRows = [ { id: "1", ref: "1.1.1", name: "Non-text Content", level: "A", principle: "Perceivable", status: "pass", notes: "All images have alt text", lastUpdated: "2025-01-10", }, { id: "2", ref: "1.2.1", name: "Audio-only and Video-only", level: "A", principle: "Perceivable", status: "todo", notes: "", lastUpdated: "2025-01-10", }, { id: "3", ref: "1.4.3", name: "Contrast (Minimum)", level: "AA", principle: "Perceivable", status: "fail", notes: "Button text fails at 3.2:1", lastUpdated: "2025-01-12", }, { id: "4", ref: "2.1.1", name: "Keyboard", level: "A", principle: "Operable", status: "pass", notes: "All interactions keyboard accessible", lastUpdated: "2025-01-11", }, { id: "5", ref: "2.4.6", name: "Headings and Labels", level: "AA", principle: "Operable", status: "pass", notes: "", lastUpdated: "2025-01-11", }, ]; export const wideColumns = [ { accessorKey: "ref", header: "Ref", size: 80 }, { accessorKey: "name", header: "Criterion", size: 220 }, { accessorKey: "level", header: "Level", size: 80 }, { accessorKey: "principle", header: "Principle", size: 140 }, { accessorKey: "status", header: "Status", size: 100 }, { accessorKey: "notes", header: "Notes", size: 220 }, { accessorKey: "lastUpdated", header: "Last updated", size: 140 }, ]; ## Basic [] = [\n { accessorKey: 'ref', header: 'Ref' },\n { accessorKey: 'name', header: 'Name' },\n { accessorKey: 'status', header: 'Status' },\n];\n\n`} > ## Multi-column sorting All columns are sortable by default. Click a header to sort ascending, click again for descending, click a third time to clear. Hold Shift to sort by multiple columns simultaneously. `} > ## Pagination Pass `pageSize` to enable built-in pagination. Previous/next controls appear below the table when the data exceeds one page. `}> ## Filtering Add `filter` to show a search input above the table that filters across all column values simultaneously. Use `filterPlaceholder` to customise the input text. `}> ## Column resizing Add `resizable` to enable drag handles on column borders. Individual columns can opt out by setting `enableResizing: false` in their column definition. `}> ## Column pinning Pass `pinnedColumns` to keep columns sticky at the left or right edge while the table scrolls horizontally. Use column `id` or `accessorKey` to identify which columns to pin. Set explicit `size` values in your column definitions for accurate sticky offsets when multiple columns are pinned. `} > ## Caption `}> ## When to use

Use DataTable when: you need multi-column sorting, pagination, column resizing, or column pinning.

Use Table instead when: {" "} your dataset is small and static — changelogs, pricing grids, blog posts. It renders server-side with zero JavaScript.

Use SummaryList instead when: {" "} you're displaying key–value pairs rather than rows of comparable records.

## Props
Prop Type Default Description
columns ColumnDef<TData>[] — TanStack Table column definitions. Re-exported as DataTableColumnDef for convenience.
data TData[] — Data rows.
caption string — Optional visible <caption> element for screen readers.
pageSize number — Rows per page. Omit to show all rows without pagination controls.
filter boolean false Show a global filter input above the table. Filters across all column values.
filterPlaceholder string "Filter…" Placeholder text for the filter input. Only used when filter is set.
resizable boolean false Enable drag-to-resize handles on column borders. Individual columns can opt out with{" "} enableResizing: false.
pinnedColumns { left?: string[]; right?: string[] } — Pin columns to the left or right edge during horizontal scroll. Pass column id{" "} or accessorKey values.

({ day: '', month: '', year: '' });\n return ;\n}`} > ## With hint text Use `hint` to provide an example date format. `} > ## Error state Pass an `error` string to show a validation message and highlight the fields. `} > ## Disabled `} > ## Props
Prop Type Default Description

hint string — Optional hint text shown below the label.
error string — Validation error message. Highlights the fields when present.
name string — Base name used to derive field names (e.g. dob-day, dob-month,{" "} dob-year).
value DateValue — Controlled value object with day, month, and year{" "} string fields.
defaultValue DateValue — Uncontrolled initial value.
onChange (value: DateValue) => void — Callback fired when any field changes.
disabled boolean false Disables all three input fields.

A modal that appears above the page content. Focus is trapped inside while open and restored to the trigger on close. Dismissed by pressing Escape or clicking outside.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { ActionDialogDemo, DismissableDialogDemo } from "../../../components/DialogDemo.tsx";

Two patterns. Use the right one for the context:

  • Action dialog — requires a decision. Footer has Cancel + primary action. No corner ✕.
  • Dismissable dialog — informational or read-only. A Dialog.Close{" "} ✕ in the top-right corner is the only close control.
## Action dialog Use when the user must make a decision — e.g. confirming a save or destructive operation. Cancel lives in the footer alongside the primary action. \n \n {(props) => }\n \n \n \n \n Edit project\n Make changes to your project settings below.\n {/* form content */}\n
\n \n {(props) => }\n \n \n
\n \n \n`} center > ## Dismissable dialog Use for informational content that requires no action. The ✕ button in the top-right corner is the sole close control. \n \n {(props) => }\n \n \n \n \n Audit summary\n A read-only summary of the latest audit run.\n ✕\n {/* informational content */}\n \n \n`} center > ## Usage notes

Always include a Dialog.Close — either in the footer (action dialog) or as the corner ✕ (dismissable dialog). Don't rely solely on Escape or click-outside; users on touch devices and screen readers need an explicit close target.

Groups related form controls under a shared legend. Provides the semantic structure required by screen readers to associate a label with a set of inputs.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { Fieldset as FieldsetDS, TextInputField } from "@eekodigital/raster";

Presentational only — compatible with any form library. Fieldset renders a native <fieldset> and <legend> and places no constraints on the children. It works seamlessly with React Hook Form, Formik, or uncontrolled inputs.

## Grouping related text inputs Use `Fieldset` to semantically group a set of related inputs under a shared label — for example, the fields that make up a postal address. `}>

Checkboxes and radios: Checkbox includes its own built-in fieldset when grouping multiple options. Use RadioGroup for radio buttons — it already renders a fieldset with its own legend. Use Fieldset when you need to group text inputs or other heterogeneous controls.

## With hint text Use the `hint` prop to add supplementary guidance below the legend. `} > ## Props
Prop Type Default Description
legend ReactNode — The visible label for the group, rendered as a <legend>.
hint string — Optional hint text displayed below the legend.
children ReactNode — The form controls to group.

Map visualisation supporting choropleth (colour regions by value) and bubble markers (sized dots at coordinates). Accepts TopoJSON data as a prop — no bundled map data. Supports mercator, equirectangular, and custom projections.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { GeoChartBasicDemo, GeoChartMarkersDemo } from "../../../components/GeoChartDemo"; ## Choropleth Regions coloured by value using a three-colour scale (red → amber → green). `}> ## With markers Bubble markers at lat/lon coordinates overlaid on the choropleth. `} >

GeoChart does not bundle any map data. Install world-atlas for world country boundaries, or provide your own TopoJSON data.

## Projections `"mercator"` (default) — most recognisable, distorts area at poles. `"equirectangular"` — honest area representation, flat appearance. Custom function: `(lon, lat) => [x, y]` where x/y are 0-1 normalised. ## Filtering regions Pass a `filter` array of feature IDs to show only specific regions: ```tsx ``` ## Props | Prop | Type | Default | Description | | --------------- | --------------------------------------- | ----------------- | ---------------------------------------- | | `topology` | `Topology` | — | TopoJSON data (e.g. from world-atlas) | | `data` | `GeoRegionDatum[]` | `[]` | `{ id, value, label? }` per region | | `colorScale` | `string[]` | Interactive scale | Colours from low to high value | | `markers` | `GeoMarker[]` | `[]` | `{ lat, lon, size?, label, color? }` | | `projection` | `"mercator" \| "equirectangular" \| fn` | `"mercator"` | Map projection | | `filter` | `string[]` | — | Feature IDs to include (show only these) | | `onRegionClick` | `(datum, id) => void` | — | Click handler per region | | `onMarkerClick` | `(marker, index) => void` | — | Click handler per marker | | `aria-label` | `string` | — | Required. |

CSS Grid layout with flexible column widths. Each Grid.Col child specifies its width using fr units, pixel values, auto, or full. No artificial column system — values pass through to grid-template-columns directly.

Prop Type Default Description
gap "1"–"24" "4" Gap between columns and rows (spacing token).
align "start" | "center" | "end" | "stretch" "stretch" Vertical alignment of items within rows.
as ElementType "div" Rendered HTML element.
className string —
### Grid.Col
Prop Type Default Description
width string "1fr" Column width: fr values, px/rem lengths,{" "} auto, or full. Accepts responsive values.
as ElementType "div" Rendered HTML element.

Line chart for showing trends over time. Supports single and multi-series, optional area fill, and automatic legend for multi-series. Zero dependencies — keyboard navigable with screen reader fallback.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { LineChartBasicDemo, LineChartMultiDemo, LineChartAreaDemo, } from "../../../components/LineChartDemo.tsx"; ## Basic `} > ## Multi-series Pass multiple series to compare trends. A legend renders automatically. `} > ## Area fill Set `area` to fill below the line. `} > ## Accessibility - SVG has `role="img"` with `aria-label` - Each series is a `role="region"` with the series name - Data points are focusable with series name, label, and value announced - Left/Right arrows navigate within a series; Up/Down switch between series - A hidden `` provides the full data for screen readers - Legend rendered as HTML (not SVG) for proper semantics ## Props
Prop Type Default Description
series LineSeries[] — Array of {`{ name, data: number[], color? }`}
labels string[] — X-axis labels (one per data point)
area boolean false Fill below the line
height number 200 Chart height in pixels
aria-label string — Required. Describes the chart.
className string —

A persistent, inline message used to communicate important status information to the user. Unlike Toast, a banner remains visible until the underlying condition is resolved.

import { NotificationBanner as NotificationBannerDS } from "@eekodigital/raster"; import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro";

Banner vs Toast. Use NotificationBanner for persistent, page-level messages that describe the current state (e.g. a form submission error or a maintenance warning). Use Toast for ephemeral confirmations of a completed action (e.g. "Report saved").

## Info \n The system will be unavailable on Saturday 29 March between 02:00 and 04:00.\n`} > The system will be unavailable on Saturday 29 March between 02:00 and 04:00. ## Success \n Your application has been received and will be reviewed within 5 working days.\n`} > Your application has been received and will be reviewed within 5 working days. ## Warning \n You have 3 reports that are past their due date.\n`} > You have 3 reports that are past their due date. ## Danger \n There was a problem saving your changes. Please try again or contact support.\n`} > There was a problem saving your changes. Please try again or contact support. ## Title only Omit `children` when a short heading is sufficient. `}> ## Body only Omit `title` for a plain message without a heading. \n Your session will expire in 5 minutes.\n`} > Your session will expire in 5 minutes. ## Props
Prop Type Default Description
variant 'info' | 'success' | 'warning' | 'danger' 'info' Controls the colour scheme and icon.
title string — Optional bold heading displayed above the body.
children ReactNode — Optional body content below the title.
role 'alert' | 'status' | 'region' 'region' ARIA live region role. Use 'alert' for urgent errors, 'status' for low-priority updates, 'region' for static contextual information.
An input for one-time codes — verification codes, 2FA codes, PINs. Each digit has its own cell. Paste support, auto-advance between cells, and mobile OTP autofill are built in.

A thin line that visually divides sections of content. Built on Radix Separator for correct semantics — decorative by default, optionally meaningful with role="separator".

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { SeparatorHorizontalDemo, SeparatorVerticalDemo, } from "../../../components/SeparatorDemo.tsx"; ## Horizontal The default orientation. Spans the full width of its container. `}> ## Vertical Set `orientation="vertical"` to divide inline content. The separator fills the height of its flex container. `}> ## Decorative vs semantic By default `decorative={true}` renders the element with `role="none"` — screen readers skip it. Set `decorative={false}` when the divider conveys meaningful structure; it will then carry `role="separator"`. ## Props
Prop Type Default Description
orientation "horizontal" | "vertical" "horizontal" Direction of the divider.
decorative boolean true When true the element is hidden from assistive technology.
className string — Additional CSS classes.

A keyboard-accessible link that allows users to jump past repeated navigation directly to the main content. Required for WCAG 2.4.1 compliance on pages with navigation blocks.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { SkipLink } from "@eekodigital/raster"; ## Default Renders a link pointing to `#main-content` with the label "Skip to main content". Normally hidden — only visible on keyboard focus. Forced visible here for demonstration. `}>

Visually hidden until focused. In production, the SkipLink is positioned off-screen and only becomes visible when a keyboard user tabs to it — the very first focusable element on the page.

## Custom href Override the default target anchor when your main landmark uses a different id. `}>
## Custom label Pass children to replace the default link text. Skip to content`}>
Skip to content

Screen readers will announce the `aria-label` ("Loading" by default) when the spinner mounts. Provide a descriptive label when context matters: `label="Submitting form"`. ## Props

Displays a structured set of key-value pairs, similar to a definition list. Commonly used on review and confirmation screens to summarise entered data before submission.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { SummaryList as SummaryListDS, SummaryListRow, SummaryListKey, SummaryListValue, SummaryListActions, } from "@eekodigital/raster"; ## Basic key/value list \n \n Full name\n Sarah Johnson\n \n \n Date of birth\n 12 March 1985\n \n \n Email address\n sarah.johnson@example.com\n \n`} > Full name Sarah Johnson Date of birth 12 March 1985 Email address sarah.johnson@example.com ## With action links Add a `SummaryListActions` cell to each row to provide contextual change links. \n \n Full name\n Sarah Johnson\n \n Change full name\n \n \n \n Date of birth\n 12 March 1985\n \n Change date of birth\n \n \n`} > Full name Sarah Johnson Change Date of birth 12 March 1985 Change ## Plain variant A lighter style with bold labels, regular-weight values, and no separator lines. \n \n Full name\n Sarah Johnson\n \n \n Date of birth\n 12 March 1985\n \n \n Email address\n sarah.johnson@example.com\n \n`} > Full name Sarah Johnson Date of birth 12 March 1985 Email address sarah.johnson@example.com ## When to use

Use SummaryList for key-value pairs on review, confirmation, or detail screens — showing a user their submitted data before they confirm, or summarising a record's attributes.

Use DataTable or Table{" "} instead {" "} when you have multiple rows of comparable records rather than a single record's fields.

## Props ### SummaryList
Prop Type Default Description
variant "default" | "plain" "default" Visual style. "plain" uses bold labels, regular-weight values, and no separator lines.
children ReactNode — One or more SummaryListRow elements.
className string — Additional CSS class names.
### SummaryListRow
Prop Type Default Description
children ReactNode — A SummaryListKey, SummaryListValue, and optionally{" "} SummaryListActions.
### SummaryListKey
Prop Type Default Description
children ReactNode — The label for the row.
### SummaryListValue
Prop Type Default Description
children ReactNode — The data value for the row.
### SummaryListActions
Prop Type Default Description
children ReactNode — Action links or buttons for the row.

A static, zero-JS table for displaying content. No sorting, filtering, or interactivity — just structured data rendered server-side. For interactive data grids use DataTable.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import { Table } from "@eekodigital/raster"; export const rows = [ { id: "1", feature: "Sorting", table: "—", dataTable: "✓" }, { id: "2", feature: "Filtering", table: "—", dataTable: "✓" }, { id: "3", feature: "Pagination", table: "—", dataTable: "✓" }, { id: "4", feature: "Zero JS", table: "✓", dataTable: "—" }, ]; export const columns = [ { key: "feature", header: "Feature", render: (r) => r.feature }, { key: "table", header: "Table", render: (r) => r.table }, { key: "dataTable", header: "DataTable", render: (r) => r.dataTable }, ]; ## Basic r.feature },\n { key: 'table', header: 'Table', render: (r) => r.table },\n { key: 'dataTable', header: 'DataTable', render: (r) => r.dataTable },\n];\n\n r.id} />`} >
r.id} /> ## Caption Pass a `caption` to render a visible `
` element that identifies the table for screen reader users. r.id} caption="Feature comparison" />`} > r.id} caption="Feature comparison" /> ## When to use

Use Table for static content — changelogs, pricing grids, comparison tables, blog posts. It renders server-side with zero JavaScript.

Use DataTable instead {" "} when you need sorting, filtering, or pagination.

Use SummaryList instead {" "} when displaying key-value pairs rather than rows of comparable data.

## Props
Prop Type Default Description
columns Column<T>[] — Column definitions. Each has key, header, and render.
rows T[] — Data rows.
getRowKey (row: T) => string — Returns a unique key for each row.
caption string — Optional visible <caption> for screen readers.

Transient notification messages that appear in the corner of the screen. Toast requires Toast.Provider and Toast.Viewport in your app root. Individual toasts are rendered by your state management layer.

import Example from "../../../components/Example.astro"; import ToastDemo from "../../../components/ToastDemo.tsx"; ## Default \n// {children}\n// \n// \n\n// Triggering a toast:\nconst [open, setOpen] = useState(false);\n\n\n\n\n Report saved\n Your changes have been saved.\n ✕\n`} center > ## Setup

Add Toast.Provider and Toast.Viewport once in your app root. The viewport is the fixed portal that renders toasts — position it after your main content.

\n \n \n \n );\n}`} lang="tsx" >

Setup code — see above.

## When to use

Use Toast for transient, non-blocking feedback — "Report saved", "Link copied", "Changes applied". The user doesn't need to act; the message auto-dismisses.

Don't use Toast for errors that require action. Use inline validation for form errors, or AlertDialog for destructive confirmations.

Don't use Toast for information the user must not miss. Toasts can be dismissed before they're read — use a persistent banner or callout instead.

## Props ### Toast.Provider
Prop Type Default Description
duration number 5000 Default auto-dismiss delay in milliseconds for all toasts.
swipeDirection 'up' | 'down' | 'left' | 'right' 'right' Direction of the swipe-to-dismiss gesture.
### Toast.Root
Prop Type Default Description
open boolean — Controlled open state.
onOpenChange (open: boolean) => void — Called when the toast opens or closes.
duration number 5000 Auto-dismiss delay in milliseconds. Overrides the provider default.
type 'foreground' | 'background' 'foreground' Accessibility hint: foreground for user-initiated actions,{" "} background for system events.
### Toast.Title, Toast.Description, Toast.Close, Toast.Action
Prop Type Default Description
children ReactNode — Content of the element.
className string — Additional CSS classes.
Contextual text that appears on hover or focus. Use for supplementary information — never for content essential to completing a task. Wrap your app in Tooltip.Provider.

Add <Tooltip.Provider> once near the root of your app (e.g. in your layout component) to control global delay and skip-delay settings. Individual tooltips don't need their own provider.

All colours are CSS custom properties defined in the theme files. Components consume semantic tokens, not raw hex values, so themes can be swapped without touching component code.

## Primitive scales Primitives are the raw palette — OKLCH-defined, perceptually uniform values that don't change between themes. Components should never reference primitives directly; use semantic tokens instead. These are shown here for reference when creating new semantic mappings. ### Scale structure (12 steps) | Steps | Role | Example usage | | ----- | ------------------------ | -------------------------------- | | 1-2 | App & subtle backgrounds | Page background, hover tint | | 3-5 | Component backgrounds | Card, input, button states | | 6-8 | Borders | Subtle, normal, strong borders | | 9-10 | Solid backgrounds | Primary button fill, badge | | 11-12 | Text | Low-contrast, high-contrast text | Steps 11-12 meet WCAG AA contrast (4.5:1) against steps 1-2. {primitiveScales.map(({ name, prefix, steps }) => (

{name}

{shadeLabels.map((shade) => (
))}
{shadeLabels.map((shade) => ( {shade} ))}
))} ## Semantic colour rules - Never use raw hex values in component or app CSS — always reference a semantic token. - Use `--color-interactive` for interactive elements (buttons, links, focus rings). Use `--color-interactive-hover` for hover and pressed states. - Status colours (`success`, `danger`, `inactive`, `warning`) each come with a `-bg` and `-border` variant. - Primitives (`--color-primitive-*`) are only for defining new semantic tokens in theme files — never use them in components.
These principles guide every design and implementation decision. They are intentionally opinionated. When in doubt, return to them.

## 1. Accessible by default Accessibility is not a feature or a compliance checklist — it is the foundation every decision is built on. Solid keyboard navigation, sensible tab order, sufficient contrast, and meaningful focus states are non-negotiable. We build an accessibility tool; we cannot be inconsistent with what we stand for. This means robust support and correct behaviour, not fancy shortcuts. ## 2. Clarity over cleverness Every element on screen earns its place. Status (Pass, Fail, N/A, To Do) — and typography are always the most visually dominant things on a page. If something doesn't communicate information or enable an action, remove it. ## 3. Do the hard work to make it simple Complexity is our problem to solve, not the user's. Simple flows, clear steps, and obvious next actions. More clicks is acceptable; confusion is not. ## 4. Be consistent, not uniform The same patterns, language, and behaviours throughout — but applied with judgement. Context shapes how a pattern is used, not whether it is used. ## 5. Errors and empty states are first-class moments Every error message says exactly what went wrong and exactly what to do next, in plain language, close to where the problem occurred. Every empty state explains why it's empty and what action moves things forward. These are not edge cases — they are part of the experience. ## 6. Designed for focus This is a tool for careful, methodical work. The design supports concentration rather than competing for attention. Colour, motion, and visual weight are used sparingly and with intent. ## 7. Every screen, every size Every screen is designed to work at every size. Desktop is the primary environment for most users, but nothing breaks, nothing is hidden, and nothing is confusing on mobile. Simple pages, single purposes, considered flows. More clicks is acceptable on mobile if each step is clear. ## 8. Minimal, but warm Simplicity is clarity, not austerity. Friendly illustrations, considered typography, and subtle warmth make the product approachable without undermining its purpose. Rounded corners, considered whitespace, and careful use of colour are welcome. Decoration for its own sake is not. > Inntrig should feel like it was designed by someone who respects both the user's time and > their intelligence — calm, warm, purposeful, and completely unshowy. ## Good UI engineering is good design We carry out principles into our code, here are some examples. ### Accessible by default Every component is built to WCAG 2.2 AA as a baseline. This means correct colour contrast, keyboard navigability, focus indicators, and proper use of HTML semantics. The first rule of ARIA: don't use ARIA when native HTML does the job. Use `

`–`

` for headings, `
Type scale, link styles, and colour usage for text across the design system.

## Type scale All sizes are defined as CSS custom properties and map to the design token scale.
Heading 1 — 3xl bold --font-size-3xl
Heading 2 — 2xl bold --font-size-2xl
Heading 3 — xl semibold --font-size-xl
Large body --font-size-lg
Base body (default) --font-size-base
Small / caption --font-size-sm
Extra small / labels --font-size-xs
## Links Text links within body copy are **always underlined**. Underlines provide a universal, colour-independent cue that text is interactive. Users who cannot distinguish the link colour from surrounding text can still identify links by the underline.

This paragraph contains an underlined link that is always visually distinct.

Nav links (breadcrumbs, top bar) suppress the underline because the interactive region is unambiguous from context. The underline is restored on `:hover`. ## Colour Link colour uses `--color-interactive` (#1d4ed8), which achieves a contrast ratio above 4.5:1 on the default white background — meeting WCAG SC 1.4.3.

## Using the design system

Install the package and import what you need. The CSS tokens must be loaded once at the root of your app.

```sh
pnpm add @eekodigital/raster
```

```ts
import "@eekodigital/raster/tokens.css"; // CSS custom properties (colours, spacing, type)
import { Button, Table, TextInputField } from "@eekodigital/raster";
```
